title: Falafel (Crispy Chickpea Fritters)

ingredients.name: dried chickpeas, soaked overnight
visual_checks: do NOT use canned — raw soaked only
ingredients.notes: do NOT use canned — raw soaked only

subs.name: 250 g dried broad beans (peeled) for Egyptian-style ta'ameya
extra coriander leaves
subs.notes: parsley adds brightness; all-coriander works fine

steps.text: Drain the soaked chickpeas well and spread on a cloth to remove excess moisture. They should be softened but still raw — do not cook them.

Combine the chickpeas, onion, garlic, coriander, parsley and green chilli in a food processor or mixie. Pulse — do not blend — until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs that just hold together when pressed. Avoid over-processing to a paste.
Transfer to a bowl. Mix in the cumin, coriander powder, baking soda, sesame seeds and salt. Refrigerate for 20 minutes — this helps the falafel hold their shape.
Shape the mixture into small flattened balls about 3 cm across. Wet your hands lightly to prevent sticking.

Heat oil in a kadai to about 175°C — a small piece of mixture should sizzle and rise to the surface in 3 seconds. Fry the falafel in batches for 3–4 minutes, turning once, until deep golden-brown all over.
Drain on paper and serve immediately — falafel are crispiest within 10 minutes of frying. Accompany with hummus, tahini or a simple tomato-cucumber salad.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chickpea → 250 g dried broad beans (peeled) for Egyptian-style ta'ameya (same process; different flavour); parsley → extra coriander leaves (parsley adds brightness; all-coriander works fine); sesame-seeds → skip entirely (optional for the crust)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Kabuli chana (white chickpeas) is widely available and inexpensive. Fry in a kadai with 400 ml oil on medium flame — a tawa wok works well too. Serve as a party snack with pudina chutney or as a stuffing inside a paratha roll.
